name: The Calibrated Authority Index
description: A living corpus coding how knowledge institutions construct trust in generative AI. Each institution's public AI policy is coded on six dimensions (D1-D6) into a composite Calibrated Authority (CA) score 0-12, plus posture, verification-boundary fit, trust-logic, and twilight markers. The observational, observed-in-the-wild evidence for the trust-formation-divergence thesis: trust forms by divergent mechanisms driven by verification economics.
measurementTechnique: Six-dimension manual coding (D1-D6, 0-2 each) of each institution's public generative-AI policy; composite Calibrated Authority score 0-12.

metadata.title: The Calibrated Authority Index
metadata.description: A living corpus coding how knowledge institutions construct trust in generative AI. Each institution's public AI policy is coded on six dimensions (D1-D6) into a composite Calibrated Authority (CA) score 0-12, plus posture, verification-boundary fit, trust-logic, and twilight markers. The observational, observed-in-the-wild evidence for the trust-formation-divergence thesis: trust forms by divergent mechanisms driven by verification economics.

fields.ca: Composite Calibrated Authority score, sum of D1-D6 (0-12).
fields.posture: The institution's overall stance toward generative AI: Prohibitive, Balanced, or Enabling.
fields.c2_fit: Verification-boundary fit: does the policy land where the thesis predicts (fits | deviates).
fields.twilight: Whether the policy uses precedent-collapse / feedback-delay / exponential-fog ('twilight') framing.

metadata.creator: Chris Huber Reitz

institutions.name: UF Center for Public Interest Communications
institutions.quote: All content must be replicable, evidence-based and traceable to sources that a human researcher can locate, review and re-create.

institutions.name: Springer Nature / Nature
institutions.quote: An attribution of authorship carries with it accountability for the work, which cannot be effectively applied to LLMs.

institutions.name: Elsevier
institutions.quote: Authorship implies responsibilities and tasks that can only be attributed to and performed by humans.

institutions.name: Wiley
institutions.quote: The author is fully responsible for the accuracy of any information provided by the AI Technology.

institutions.name: Taylor & Francis
institutions.quote: Generative AI tools must not be listed as an author because such tools are unable to assume responsibility for the submitted content or manage copyright and licensing agreements. These are uniquely human responsibilities that cannot be undertaken by Generative AI tools.

institutions.name: Science / AAAS
institutions.quote: An AI program cannot be an author of a Science journal paper.

institutions.name: ICMJE
institutions.quote: Chatbots (such as ChatGPT) should not be listed as authors because they cannot be responsible for the accuracy, integrity, and originality of the work, and these responsibilities are required for authorship.

institutions.name: COPE
institutions.quote: AI tools cannot meet the requirements for authorship as they cannot take responsibility for the submitted work. As non-legal entities, they cannot assert the presence or absence of conflicts of interest nor manage copyright and license agreements.

institutions.name: PLOS
institutions.quote: The use of AI tools and technologies to fabricate or otherwise misrepresent primary research data is unacceptable.

institutions.name: Poynter Institute
institutions.quote: All information generated by AI requires human verification. Everything we publish will live up to our long-time standards of verification.

institutions.name: Reuters Institute (Oxford)
institutions.quote: You cannot simply 'hack' your way to trust.

institutions.name: Columbia / Tow Center
institutions.quote: We won't publish a story if our only source is AI — it's not a substitute for the careful review of journalists.

institutions.name: Associated Press
institutions.quote: Any output from a generative AI tool should be treated as unvetted source material.
institutions.quote_label: Standards around generative AI (via Poynter/Globe reprint)

institutions.name: Harvard University
institutions.quote: You are responsible for any content that you produce or publish that includes AI-generated material.

institutions.name: Stanford University
institutions.quote: Absent a clear statement from a course instructor, use of or consultation with generative AI shall be treated analogously to assistance from another person.

institutions.name: MIT
institutions.quote: You are responsible for the accuracy of any information you publish, including AI-generated content.

institutions.name: University of Michigan
institutions.quote: All data shared with U-M's AI services is private and will not be used to train AI models.

institutions.name: Russell Group (UK)
institutions.quote: Accountability for the accuracy of information generated by these tools when transferred to another context lies with the user.
institutions.quote_label: Principles on generative AI in education

institutions.name: MLA-CCCC Task Force
institutions.quote: We and others would caution against using LLMs to assess student writing or to write tailored feedback to students, given the danger of undermining trust and human connection in the classroom.

institutions.name: ACM
institutions.quote: When using Artificial Intelligence to assist with writing an ACM submission, ACM no longer requires the disclosure of information regarding the use of AI.

provenance.note: Policy updated 2026-05-14: AI writing-assistance disclosure no longer required (research-use still must be described in Methods). D3 2->1; CA 10->11.

institutions.name: APA
institutions.quote: AI is not a conscious human who can consent to the duties and responsibilities of authorship, which include responsibility for post publication changes such as corrections or retractions.

institutions.name: Yale University
institutions.quote: Always review and verify outputs generated by AI tools, especially before publication. We are each responsible for the content of our work product.

institutions.name: Princeton University
institutions.quote: Generative AI is not a source as defined in this provision because its output is not created by a person.

institutions.name: University of California, Berkeley
institutions.quote: No personal, confidential, proprietary, or otherwise sensitive information may be entered into or generated as output from models.

institutions.name: Cornell University
institutions.quote: You are accountable for your work, regardless of the tools you use to produce it.

institutions.name: Carnegie Mellon University
institutions.quote: You are ultimately responsible for the content that you submit.

institutions.name: Georgia Institute of Technology
institutions.quote: Core scholarly and research contributions are expected to remain under the full direction and responsibility of the GT community member.

institutions.name: University of Toronto
institutions.quote: Generative AI tools do not meet the criteria for authorship of scholarly works, because these tools cannot take responsibility or be held accountable for submitted work.

institutions.name: University of Cambridge
institutions.quote: A student using any unacknowledged content generated by artificial intelligence within a summative assessment as though it is their own work constitutes academic misconduct.

institutions.name: University College London
institutions.quote: The student should still be the author of their own work — GenAI should be limited to supporting and assisting the student.

institutions.name: Arizona State University
institutions.quote: Any submitted course assignment that does not explicitly articulate how generative AI was used will be assumed to have been created entirely without its use.

institutions.name: Cambridge University Press
institutions.quote: AI does not meet the Cambridge requirements for authorship, given the need for accountability.

institutions.name: Oxford University Press
institutions.quote: Gen AI does not qualify as an author and should not be used to undertake primary authorial responsibilities, such as generating arguments and scientific insights, writing analysis, or drawing conclusions.

institutions.name: SAGE Publishing
institutions.quote: We distinguish various uses for AI: assistive (no longer requiring disclosure), generative (requiring disclosure), and prohibitive.

institutions.name: IEEE
institutions.quote: The use of content generated by artificial intelligence in an article shall be disclosed in the acknowledgments section.

institutions.name: PNAS
institutions.quote: The software cannot be listed as an author because it does not meet the criteria for authorship and cannot share responsibility for the paper or be held accountable for the integrity of the data reported.

institutions.name: JAMA Network
institutions.quote: Attribution of authorship carries with it accountability for the work, and AI tools cannot take such responsibility.

institutions.name: New England Journal of Medicine
institutions.quote: Because the authors of a manuscript are responsible for the accuracy, integrity, and originality of the work, chatbots or other AI-assisted technologies cannot be listed as authors.

institutions.name: World Association of Medical Editors
institutions.quote: In the interests of enabling scientific scrutiny, including replication and identifying falsification, the full prompt used to generate the research results, the time and date of query, and the AI tool used and its version, should be provided.

institutions.name: Council of Science Editors
institutions.quote: A nonhuman cannot be responsible or accountable for the accuracy, integrity, and originality of the work.
institutions.quote_label: Guidance on machine learning and AI tools

institutions.name: The Royal Society
institutions.quote: Such systems must not replace key researcher tasks such as producing scientific insights, analysing and interpreting data.

institutions.name: BBC
institutions.quote: BBC use of AI must never undermine the trust of audiences.

institutions.name: Reuters (news agency)
institutions.quote: Our images and stories must reflect reality.

institutions.name: The Guardian
institutions.quote: Guardian audiences are entitled to expect that work that appears under your byline has been authored by you.

institutions.name: The New York Times
institutions.quote: We don't use A.I. to write articles, and journalists are ultimately responsible for everything that we publish.

institutions.name: Society of Professional Journalists
institutions.quote: Take responsibility for the accuracy of their work. Verify information before releasing it. Use original sources whenever possible.

institutions.name: Association of Research Libraries
institutions.quote: Libraries believe 'no human, no AI.'

institutions.name: EDUCAUSE
institutions.quote: [The policy creates] an untenable situation for students who must somehow defend themselves against a machine that cannot show its work but is just a projection.
institutions.quote_label: Cross-Campus Approaches to a GenAI Policy

institutions.name: UNESCO — GenAI in education & research
institutions.quote: A human-centred approach requires proper regulation that can ensure human agency, transparency and public accountability.
institutions.quote_label: Guidance for generative AI in education and research

institutions.name: OECD AI Principles
institutions.quote: AI actors should ensure traceability, including in relation to datasets, processes and decisions made during the AI system lifecycle, to enable analysis of the AI system's outputs and responses to inquiry.

institutions.name: US Dept of Education (OET)
institutions.quote: A top priority with AI is to keep humans in the loop and in control.
institutions.quote_label: AI and the Future of Teaching and Learning

institutions.name: Vatican (DDF/DCE) — Antiqua et Nova (2025)
institutions.quote: Between a machine and a human being, only the latter is truly a moral agent.
